cover.title: THE GEM
cover.subtitle: The one who sees what could be better — and says so.
cover.tagline: You don't see what's lacking. You see what could be refined.

profile_scores.caption: High drive and expression, low warmth in delivery — you see the spot that could get better, and you say it exactly as it is.

short_summary: You don't see what's lacking in people — you see what could be refined, and you say so right away.
The loneliness you've felt wasn't about loving precision too much. It was that no one ever realized precision was your way of loving.
What's still missing isn't softer eyes. It's one more sentence that shows your sharp words come from seeing someone's potential.

full_report.headline: THE GEM
full_report.intro: You say what you see. When something could get better but stops right there, you don't let it go — you point it out precisely. This report follows what that precision costs you in love: why it gets heard as criticism, what's actually missing, and what changes it.

chapters.title: When You Love
body: You're someone who says what you see. If something could get better but stops right there, you don't just let it go — you point it out precisely. People call you sharp because of that. They call you picky too. Sometimes they call you cold.

But here's the thing — you're not someone who looks for what's lacking. You're someone who sees the part that could get even better. It's the same when you love. When you see that just a little more polish would make things so much better, you say it right away, because you see the potential.
But the people on the receiving end only ever hear it as "you're saying I'm not enough again." Actually, you weren't angry — you were just a little disappointed.
chapters.profile_note: High drive and expression, low warmth in delivery — You see the spot that could get better, and you say it exactly as it is.

chapters.title: The Pattern You Repeat
body: You're an attractive person early in a relationship, because you tell the truth and there's no empty flattery. So the other person trusts you. The problem starts after that.

You're not someone who looks for what's lacking — you're someone who sees the part that could get even better. The problem is the other person doesn't know the difference. They don't feel like you're showing them potential; they feel like they're being criticized for falling short. So they start trying less, and you point that out precisely too: "You don't seem to be putting in effort lately." But that wasn't them losing interest — it was them giving up. You were accurate, but you missed the most important signal of all.
chapters.highlight: The reason you feel lonely isn't that you love precision. It's that no one ever realized that precision was love.

chart.title: THE NEVER ENOUGH CURVE
chart.description: As the relationship deepens, your wish to refine things further stays the same, the other person's attempts keep shrinking, and you keep precisely pointing out that shrinking.

chapters.title: What You Got Wrong About Yourself

items.truth: That's partly true. But you're not just being honest — you're someone who believes that noticing what could be refined further is itself a form of love. But the other person might not know how to take that precision as affection.
items.truth_short: You're not just honest — you believe noticing potential is love. The other person may not read precision as affection.

items.belief: You should listen to what's true.
items.truth: Well, that's true. But even true things have timing and delivery. Being right and being helpful in that moment are two different things.
items.truth_short: Being right and being helpful in the moment aren't the same thing.

items.belief: If I let it slide, I lose myself.
items.truth: No. That's not telling you to see less. It's telling you to show, through your delivery, that the precision is interest in their potential, not criticism.
items.truth_short: It's not about seeing less — it's about showing your precision is interest, not criticism.

chapters.title: The Person You Keep Choosing
body: They're all different people. But strangely, people with a similar texture keep coming into your life — someone who settles for "good enough," someone who could clearly get much better but stops right there. That always feels like a waste to you — not because they lack ability, but because they give up right when just a little more polish would do it.
chapters.highlight: Someone who acknowledges you finds your gaze burdensome. Someone who loves you knows you're seeing their potential.

chapters.title: What Actually Changes Something
body: What you need isn't to see less. That's who you are at your core, and there's no need to dial it down.

What you actually need is to add one more sentence after you point something out. Don't stop at "this part is a bit lacking." Go all the way to "but I think it'd be really great if you polished it just a little more."
chapters.action_sentence: That one sentence is the only signal that separates criticism from potential.

chapters.title: Where to Go From Here
body: You already know how to spot the place that could get better. You know how to say it accurately.
But what you'll learn next is how to show that your gaze comes from seeing potential.
chapters.transition_note: This isn't about being less precise. It's actually the opposite — it's about your sincerity getting through more accurately.

chapters.title: The Type You Keep Falling For

items.description: Just a little more would do it, but they stop there and settle. That stopping point feels like such a waste to you.

items.type_title: Someone Who Chooses Comfort Over Growth
items.description: The process of refining things is tiring, so they'd rather just leave things as they are. That feels like a shame to you.

items.type_title: Someone Who Thinks "Close Enough" Is Fine
items.description: Once it's almost right, that's it for them. For you, "almost" and "exactly" are completely different words — but to this person, they're the same word.

items.type_title: Someone Who Doesn't Care Much About Detail
items.description: They only look at the big picture and stop there. This person doesn't really believe that small differences end up changing the whole thing.

chapters.title: The Warning Signs You've Missed
chapters.intro_note: These signs actually showed up pretty early. It's just that every time, you only pointed them out more precisely.

signal_strength.label: After hearing what you point out, the other person's words gradually shrink
You mistake that for "they must be trying to do better"
They reduce their attempts altogether, because the process of refining feels scary
You point that out accurately too, but you miss that it's actually a sign of giving up

chapters.core_signal: The other person thinks you're angry, but actually you were just disappointed.

chapters.title: What a Healthy Relationship Looks Like to You

checklist.title: They understand the heart behind your gaze.
checklist.description: They know it's not criticism — it's you seeing potential.

checklist.title: They don't fall apart hearing something true.
checklist.description: They don't get defensive. They listen, and they accept it.

checklist.title: They take precision as affection.
checklist.description: Inside the sharp words, they know there's a heart that sees potential.

checklist.title: Being together makes things more refined.
checklist.description: This is the most important part — a relationship where both sides refine each other, not one where only one side does the refining.

chapters.title: What Happens in Your Next Relationship

sections.who_breaks_you: You almost never fail to spot where something could get better. The problem is that gaze always comes across as criticism. So there's someone who doesn't fit you well: someone who only hears your words as criticism and reduces their attempts. They talk less and less, try less and less. You point that out accurately too, but you fail to read that it's actually a sign of giving up.
sections.who_steadies_you: On the other hand, there's someone who unexpectedly fits you well: someone who accepts that your gaze is disappointment, not anger. Someone who, when you point something out, asks back, "So you were just disappointed, right?" In front of someone like that, you don't need to keep adding explanations every single time.
sections.same_pattern_repeats: You might be drawn again to someone who settles for "good enough." You might fall for someone who doesn't care much about detail. That can't really be stopped — that's just who you are. The problem isn't being drawn to them — it's how long you keep showing only your gaze without showing your heart, after that.
sections.next_signs: Remember the signs from Chapter 8? They usually didn't show up until months in. This time, they'll show up faster, and smaller. The moment, right as you're about to point something out, when their words get just slightly shorter than usual — that's where it starts.
sections.try_this: Normally, you would've just pointed it out and stopped there. This time, don't. Just once. After you point it out, add: "But I think it'd be really great if you polished it just a little more." Then watch their reaction. Someone who can take it listens without getting defensive. Someone who can't still only hears it as criticism.

action_plan.summary: You already know how to see what could be better. What's left is showing that the seeing comes from care, not criticism.
action_items: Next time you point something out, add one sentence: what you think it could become, not just what's missing.
Notice when someone's words start getting shorter — that's often a quieter sign of giving up, not improving.
Ask yourself before you speak: am I about to name a gap, or name a possibility?

closing.message: You've spent a long time learning to see and say where things could get better. This report covered why your precision has so often been misread as criticism, and how you can deliver the heart behind it more accurately.
closing.cross_sell_cta: Curious what your partner's pattern looks like? See their Cosmic Blueprint.
